title: Local rigidity and C^0 symplectic and contact topology
speaker: Mike Usher
abstract: I will explain how coisotropic submanifolds of symplectic manifolds can be distinguished among all submanifolds by a criterion ("local rigidity") related to the Hofer energy necessary to disjoin open sets from them. This criterion is invariant under symplectic homeomorphisms, leading to a simplified proof of the Humiliere-Leclercq-Seyfaddini theorem that a symplectic-homeomorphic image of a coisotropic submanifold that is smooth is coisotropic.

proof_stages.label: C^0 symplectic rigidity landscape and target theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Eliashberg-Gromov C^0 rigidity for symplectomorphisms, the definition of symplectic homeomorphism, Laudenbach-Sikorav rigidity for Lagrangian embeddings, and the Humiliere-Leclercq-Seyfaddini theorem that a smooth image of a coisotropic under a symplectic homeomorphism is coisotropic.

proof_stages.label: Hofer local rigidity of subsets
proof_stages.summary: A closed set is called rigid if every open set meeting it has positive Hofer energy of displacement from it, and a locally closed set is locally rigid if this holds after restricting to suitable neighborhoods of each point.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to characterization plus invariance
proof_stages.summary: The desired coisotropic-image theorem is reduced to two assertions: for smooth submanifolds, coisotropy is equivalent to local rigidity, and local rigidity is invariant under symplectic homeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Invariance under symplectic homeomorphisms
proof_stages.summary: If C is rigid and ψ is a C^0 limit of symplectomorphisms ψ_n, then any Hamiltonian disjoining an open set from ψ(C) can be conjugated by ψ_n to disjoin a corresponding open set from C, and conjugation-invariance of the Hofer norm transfers the same positive lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Non-coisotropic submanifolds are not locally rigid
proof_stages.summary: For a non-coisotropic submanifold, a symplectic-normal vector outside the tangent space yields Hamiltonians vanishing on the submanifold whose vector fields immediately move off it, and cutoffs make the disjoining Hofer energy arbitrarily small.

proof_stages.label: Local rigidity of arbitrary Lagrangians
proof_stages.summary: A previously proved Floer-theoretic rigidity theorem for closed Lagrangians supplies positive displacement energy, and the locality of the definition together with Darboux-Weinstein charts transports this to every local Lagrangian.

proof_stages.label: Coisotropic local rigidity from Lagrangian slices
proof_stages.summary: Through each point of a coisotropic submanifold there is a small Lagrangian contained in it, so disjoining a neighborhood from the coisotropic is at least as hard as disjoining it from that Lagrangian, completing the equivalence between coisotropy and local rigidity and hence the new proof of the theorem.

proof_stages.label: Contact analogues and conformal-factor obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The contact section compares analogues of the three symplectic theorems, replaces Hofer’s norm by Shelukhin’s contact norm, and explains that the lack of conjugation-invariance leaves a coisotropic-image result only under a non-collapse lower bound on the conformal factors of the approximating contactomorphisms.

examples.name: Shear homeomorphism of the symplectic plane
examples.note: The map (x,y)↦(x,y+f(x)) on R^2, with f merely continuous, makes concrete how a nonsmooth homeomorphism can be a C^0 limit of genuine symplectomorphisms.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker gives the shear map explicitly and explains how approximating the continuous function by smooth functions produces symplectic approximants

examples.name: Legendrian zigzag approximation of a knot
examples.note: A smooth knot in R^3 can be C^0-approximated by inserting Legendrian zigzags, giving the visible reason that the direct contact analogue of the Lagrangian C^0 theorem fails.
